text: For the setup, we positioned the patient in a lateral decubitus under general anesthesia and one lung ventilation.
The lobectomy was performed by an anterior four centimeter subcostal incision and a posterior subcostal accessory port as we can see in this image.
The anterior incision was performed parallel to the right costal arch.
Then a subcostal tunnel was created by blunt dissection first and under endoscopic vision after until accessing the pleural cavity.
This step was carefully carried out by the subcostal plane, avoiding entering the abdominal cavity.
To do so, the abdominal muscles were divided until the transverse abdominalis fascia was visible.
At that point, blunt dissection was carried out in the direction of the pleural cavity.
and the tunneling below the costal cartilages continued using the cautery under direct vision until the pleura was reached and opened.
Once the incision is opened, a wound retractor is placed to keep it open and dry during the surgery.
A second subcostal incision is performed posteriorly below the 10th rib and it's used to insert a 12mm trochlea under endoscopic vision.

The laser indicator was targeted to the most lateral angle of the anterior subcostal incision, and then the robotic arm number one was extended caudally and posteriorly, while the rest of the three arms were extended caudally and anteriorly.
The docking of the camera was done first in arm number two in a 30 degrees down setting and placed at the posterior angle of the anterior incision.
Following arm number three is docked on position in the middle of the subcostal wound next to the camera.
And finally arm number four is docked and placed on the medial angle of the incision.
Once the arms were in position, a tip-up fenestrated forceps was placed in arm number one with a left hand setting.
This was the arm placed in the posterior 12 millimeter port and was used also to insert the robotic staplers.
A Maryland forceps was placed in arm number three, also in a left hand setting, and the hook was equipped in arm number four, controlled by the surgeon's right hand.
For the lobectomy, we performed an anterior fissureless technique.
Firstly, the lung was retracted posteriorly and inferiorly, and the anterior and superior aspect of the hilar pleura was opened parallel to the phrenic bundle and the azimuth vein.
Then the upper lobe vein was dissected.
A vessel loop was used to help positioning the robotic curved tip vascular stapler that would use to divide the vein.
Following, the truncus anterior branch of the pulmonary artery was identified and dissected out.
The lymph node of the station 10R was identified and then it was resected out to achieve a safer positioning of the staper while transecting the anterior branch.
After the pulmonary artery branch was divided using the same technique that we used with the vein.
The range of movement provided by the robotic stapers together with the curved tip are very appreciated while dividing these delicate structures.
Next, the upper lobe bronchus was identified and dissected.
During the surgery, the assistant surgeon used a long-curve suction allowing for smoke-free vision and facilitating some steps of the procedure with it as well.
Due to the angles and the lack of a curved tip staper to complete this step, passing a vessel lobe helps to get the staper positioned.
The bronchus is divided using a green load 60 millimeter staper.
Here we can see how the posterior ascending artery to the right upper lobe was identified and dissected.
For our surprise, in this case, an accessory ascending branch for the anterior segment was identified.

Both arterial branches were carefully dissected, and in this case, it was possible to divide both vessels with the use of a curve-tips taper with a vascular load that was carefully positioned, and both vessels were transected at the same time.
Once the lower structures were divided, staplers were then used to divide the minor and major fissure in order to complete the lobectomy.
One of the advantages of the X-eye system compared with the previous versions is that the docking is very fast and versatile.
This allows us to easily interchange instruments, camera and trockers as required during the procedure.
We used black, green, or blue loads according to the thickness of the tissue.
Once the lobectomy is completed, the specimen was positioned at the base of the cavity to allow for lymph node assessment.
Here we can appreciate how station two and four are reachable with this approach.
Next, station seven lymph node is also dissected and sampled.
Following, the specimen was positioned at the apex of the cavity to allow dividing the right inferior pulmonary ligament and resecting the lymph node station 9R.
The specimen was removed using an endoscopic bag.
Here we can see we performed a thoracic paravertebral block
Finally, a drain was inserted by the posterior port and lung expansion was confirmed.
